issue: The feature-flags API route reads and WRITES data/feature-flags/features.json directly with fs.readFile/fs.writeFile, bypassing the feature-flags-core / MCP abstraction. CLAUDE.md mandates that flag mutations go ONLY through the feature-flags MCP tools and that features.json is NEVER edited directly. This route IS the direct-edit path and is the single source of persistence for the whole flag system, so every consumer (admin action, stdio MCP, HTTP wrapper) funnels file mutation through this one unguarded layer.
adr_violated: CLAUDE.md project rule (M3 MCP Usage); no ADR documents the flag-storage architecture
recommendation: Extract a feature-flags-core persistence/repository module (readFlags/writeFlag) that owns the JSON file, and have this route + MCP servers call it. Document the file-as-store decision in a new ADR (see ADR-0004 proposal). At minimum, centralize the read-modify-write so the file is never touched in two places.

issue: A 'use server' admin action makes a server-to-server HTTP fetch back into the same Next.js app's own /api/feature-flags route instead of calling shared domain logic in feature-flags-core. This is the app calling itself over the network: it crosses a process/transport boundary for an in-process operation, duplicates auth (Clerk admin check here, x-api-key check in the route), and couples the action to a self URL (NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL as an internal target).
evidence: const res = await fetch(`${internalUrl}/api/feature-flags`, { method: "PATCH", headers: { "x-api-key": apiKey, ... } })
adr_violated: none (layering convention in task brief: Next app -> server actions -> db layer)
recommendation: Move flag read/validate/write into feature-flags-core and call it directly from the server action and from the route handler. Keep the HTTP route only as the boundary for OUT-OF-PROCESS consumers (stdio MCP, n8n), not for the app calling itself.

issue: Two divergent, hardcoded PLAN_TOKEN_MAP constants are the source of truth for how many tokens a plan grants, and they DISAGREE. stripe.ts maps plan '10'->20 and '5'->60; user.ts maps '5'->20 and '10'->60. checkoutCredits embeds yet a third copy in Stripe metadata. The same conceptual decision (plan->credits) is encoded in 3 places with no shared definition, so a purchase grants different credits depending on which path computes it.
evidence: stripe.ts: const PLAN_TOKEN_MAP = { "10": 20, "5": 60 }  // user.ts: const PLAN_TOKEN_MAP = { "5": 20, "10": 60 }
recommendation: Define a single billing/credits map in one module (e.g. packages/api-types or a billing-core) and import it in stripe.ts, user.ts, and checkoutCredits. This is both an integrity bug and a maintenance hazard. (Cross-refs security-review HIGH on forged-metadata credit grants.)

issue: getReportById queries ReportsTable by id only, with NO userId predicate and NO auth() call, while sibling actions (addRemoveFavorite, deleteReportFromDB, getReports) all apply and(eq(id), eq(userId)). ADR-0001 states RLS is DISABLED and authorization is enforced 'purely at the application layer via Drizzle where(eq(userId, currentUser))'. This action is the documented authorization boundary and it omits the filter, so the ADR's only access-control mechanism is bypassed -> IDOR. saveReport (line 41) is the mirror write-side problem: it trusts a caller-supplied userId with no auth() check.
evidence: const report = await db.query.ReportsTable.findFirst({ where: eq(ReportsTable.id, reportId) });
adr_violated: ADR-0001 (Supabase + Drizzle): 'we must explicitly disable RLS ... and enforce authorization purely at the application layer via Drizzle queries (where(eq(userId, currentUser)))'
recommendation: Add await auth() and where: and(eq(ReportsTable.id, reportId), eq(ReportsTable.userId, userId)). For saveReport, derive userId from auth() not the argument. This overlaps security-review HIGH #3 and is ALSO an ADR-0001 layering violation, so it should block.

issue: createTransaction (and updateCredits in user.ts:101) mutate balances for an arbitrary caller-supplied buyerId/userId with no auth() and no ownership check, violating ADR-0001's mandate that ALL access control lives in the app layer via where(eq(userId, currentUser)). Because RLS is off, these unauthenticated writes have no database-level backstop. The decision to make these 'use server' actions callable with any userId is undocumented and contradicts the only authz model the project has.
evidence: await db.insert(TransactionsTable).values({ plan: transaction.plan, userId: transaction.buyerId });
adr_violated: ADR-0001 (RLS disabled, authz at app layer only)
recommendation: These should not be public server actions taking a userId. Make them internal functions invoked only by the verified Stripe webhook (after signature verification), keyed off webhook-trusted metadata; never expose a userId-parameterized credit mint to the client. Overlaps security-review HIGH #2.

issue: Token-balance business logic (read tokens, gate on 0, decrement, persist) lives inline in the API route handler with a direct Drizzle read-modify-write, duplicated almost verbatim in follow-up-claude/route.ts (which decrements by 2 and gates differently). There is no service/db-layer function owning 'spend N tokens'. This is business logic in the controller layer, and the duplication has already drifted (different costs, different gate conditions).
evidence: const updatedTokens = user.tokens - 1; await db.update(UserTable).set({ tokens: updatedTokens }).where(eq(UserTable.id, user.id))
recommendation: Extract a spendTokens(userId, cost) function in the server/db layer that does an atomic conditional decrement, and call it from both routes. Fixes the layering violation and the read-then-write race (latter is security/perf-mate's concern).

issue: packages/api-types declares itself 'the source of truth for the wire format' shared by desktop collector, app backend, and frontend, and is listed as a workspace dependency in both apps/app and apps/landing package.json. But NO TypeScript file anywhere imports it (verified by grep). Meanwhile the app validates trades with its own apps/app/src/zodSchema/schema.ts (newTradeFormSchema), and the field names have already drifted: api-types uses symbolName/instrumentName/positionType while the live schema/contract differ. A 'source of truth' that nothing consumes is a premature abstraction that will rot and mislead.
evidence: TradeInputSchema in api-types is never imported; apps use zodSchema/schema.ts newTradeFormSchema instead. grep for '@tradewitness/api-types' in *.ts/*.tsx returns zero hits.
adr_violated: ADR-0003 (Turborepo) cites 'share core TypeScript interfaces (Zod schemas for User and Trade)' via packages/api-types as the JUSTIFICATION for the monorepo; the package being unused undermines that decision
recommendation: Either wire the app's trade validation + the (still-missing) desktop-collector ingest route to import from api-types and make it genuinely canonical, or delete the package and drop the dependency. Do not ship a dormant 'source of truth' alongside a divergent real one.

issue: The Stripe webhook is the integration boundary for an external system, yet on constructEvent failure it returns NextResponse.json({...}) with an implicit HTTP 200 (fail-open) and then, on success, hands attacker-influenceable metadata (buyerId, credits) straight to createTransaction. The contract between Stripe and the credit-granting domain is undefined: no typed event schema, no signature-failure -> 4xx contract, no idempotency key. This is an event-driven integration without a contract.
evidence: } catch (err) { return NextResponse.json({ message: "Webhook error", error: err }); }  // no status -> 200
adr_violated: none (no ADR documents webhook/payment integration boundaries)
recommendation: Define a typed, validated event contract; return 400 on verification failure (fail closed); pass only verified fields into the (internalized) credit-grant function; add idempotency on stripeId. Overlaps security-review HIGH #1.

issue: validateStateChange dependency-graph logic is reimplemented by hand in the stdio MCP server (index.ts), while packages/feature-flags-core already exports a DependencyGraph class with validateStateChange/validateTrafficChange that the HTTP wrapper (http.ts) and the Next route both use. Three components, two divergent implementations of the same invariant. The hand-rolled version in index.ts can drift from the canonical core rules (e.g. it lacks the 'dependency not found' branch the core has).
evidence: mcps/feature-flags/src/index.ts defines its own validateStateChange(); http.ts uses new DependencyGraph(flags).validateStateChange()
recommendation: Delete the bespoke validateStateChange in the stdio MCP and use DependencyGraph from feature-flags-core, matching http.ts. Single implementation of the dependency invariant.

issue: The embedding provider abstraction (getBgeEmbedder + getEmbedding with the openai/bge-m3 branch, plus the dotenv/Qdrant client bootstrap) is copy-pasted verbatim across three files: mcps/rag/ingest.ts, mcps/rag/query.ts, and mcps/search-docs/src/index.ts. Embedding model choice and vector size (1024 vs 1536) are a cross-cutting decision duplicated in 3 places; a model change requires editing all three and they can silently disagree (e.g. collection ingested at one size, queried with another).
evidence: Identical getEmbedding()/getBgeEmbedder() blocks in mcps/rag/ingest.ts, mcps/rag/query.ts, mcps/search-docs/src/index.ts
recommendation: Extract an embeddings module (e.g. packages/rag-core or mcps/rag/lib/embeddings.ts) exporting getEmbedding + VECTOR_SIZE + the Qdrant client factory, and import it everywhere. Prevents dimension/model drift between ingest and query.

issue: A hardcoded fallback secret 'local-m3-change-me' is the shared auth token, and the SAME literal is duplicated as a magic string in four places: this route, mcps/feature-flags/src/index.ts:9, mcps/feature-flags/src/http.ts:8, and the admin action actions.ts:10. The auth contract between the app and its MCP/HTTP consumers is encoded as a copy-pasted string constant with no single definition, so rotating it means editing four files.
evidence: const DEV_API_KEY = "local-m3-change-me"; ... returns DEV_API_KEY when NODE_ENV !== 'production'
recommendation: Centralize the dev key (and the env-var name) in feature-flags-core or a shared config module; require the env var to be set rather than silently defaulting. Cross-refs security-review HIGH on the hardcoded shared secret.

issue: Internal server-to-server call falls back to NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL as its target. NEXT_PUBLIC_* values are client-exposed and represent the public origin, which may differ from the internal/in-cluster address; using a public env var as an internal transport target couples the internal call path to public routing and can break behind proxies/edge. Same pattern in the two MCP servers.
recommendation: Prefer a dedicated internal-URL env (APP_INTERNAL_URL) and avoid falling back to a NEXT_PUBLIC_ value for server-side calls; better yet, remove the self-HTTP call entirely per the layering finding above.

issue: getAllTradeRecords manually strips userId and coerces ~15 nullable DB columns to undefined inline in the action to massage the Drizzle row shape into the frontend Trades type. This DB-row-to-DTO mapping is hand-maintained boilerplate that leaks the persistence shape into the action layer and must be updated by hand whenever a column is added; it is exactly the kind of mapping packages/api-types was meant to standardize.
evidence: const processedData = data.map(({ userId, ...t }) => ({ ...t, notes: t.notes ?? undefined, result: t.result ?? undefined, /* 13 more */ }))
adr_violated: ADR-0003 (api-types intended to share Trade types) — see also the api-types orphan finding
recommendation: Define a single toTradeDTO(row) mapper (ideally validated by a shared api-types schema) and reuse it. Keeps the persistence->wire mapping in one place.

issue: The Claude system prompt and JSON-extraction/parsing pipeline are hardcoded inline in the route handler (and a second variant inline in follow-up-claude with a different model: claude-sonnet-4-5 vs claude-3-7-sonnet). There is no LLM client/service abstraction, so model selection, prompt, and response-parsing strategy are scattered across route files with no shared contract for the AI response shape (claudeComments structure exists only as prose in the prompt).
evidence: system: 'Using the provided trade data, group each trade...' inline; model differs between the two routes
adr_violated: none (the AI-reports decision in docs/architecture-decisions.md sec 5 actually says to CUT paid Anthropic API calls for MVP — these routes contradict that WIP decision, worth confirming)
recommendation: Introduce an LLM service module owning model id, prompts, and a Zod-validated response schema; share it between both routes. Also reconcile with architecture-decisions.md sec 5 (BYO-AI / no paid API) — either update that doc or the code.

note: ADR-0002 (R2 via aws4fetch, no @aws-sdk/client-s3): apps/app/src/lib/r2.ts correctly uses AwsClient from aws4fetch and buffers the body as required; no AWS SDK import. Compliant.
